title: Inflight-coalesced get() counts hit/miss twice for the same underlying disk read
description: When a second caller joins an already-in-flight get (line 119-122), it increments hitCount or missCount. The original caller (lines 129-130) also increments hitCount or missCount when the same shared promise resolves. One disk read therefore inflates both counters by 2, making hitRate computed in stats() systematically wrong under concurrent load. The same pattern exists in node.ts lines 81-83 vs 90-91.
evidence: Verified in browser.ts lines 118-130: the joining caller increments hitCount/missCount at line 121, and the original caller also increments at line 130 — same promise, two stat increments. Same pattern confirmed in node.ts lines 79-91. hitRate is systematically inflated under concurrent load.

title: node.ts get() does not await inflight set() before reading from disk, risking stale read
description: browser.ts get() explicitly awaits any in-flight set for the same key (lines 115-116) before falling through to getPersistent, ensuring the disk write lands before the read. node.ts get() has no equivalent guard: it proceeds directly to getPersistent (line 86) while a concurrent set() is still writing a tmp file and renaming it. A race between the rename and the readFile can return undefined (NotFoundError) when the data was in fact just written, inflating miss counts and causing cache misses that should be hits.
evidence: Verified: browser.ts lines 115-116 await inflightSets before falling through; node.ts lines 63-96 have no such guard (no reference to inflightSets in get()). A concurrent set() that is mid-rename will cause getPersistent() to get ENOENT and return undefined, causing a spurious miss. This is also reported as contracts-9.

title: getPersistent returns the same ArrayBuffer reference stored in memoryCache, allowing caller mutation to corrupt the cache
description: getPersistent stores `master` in memoryCache (line 108) and then returns `master` directly (line 110). The caller at get() returns this reference to upstream code. If the caller mutates the buffer, the copy held in memoryCache is corrupted. Compare to browser.ts getPersistent line 271 which correctly returns `buffer.slice(0)` — a fresh copy — while keeping the original in the memory cache. The node.ts path skips that defensive copy on the persistent-read hot path.
evidence: Confirmed in node.ts lines 107-110: `master` is stored in memoryCache at line 108 and returned directly at line 110. Browser.ts line 271 correctly returns `buffer.slice(0)` while keeping `buffer` in the cache. Caller mutation of the returned buffer corrupts the cached copy silently. Also flagged as performance-4.

title: delete() increments evictionsCount for user-initiated deletes, inflating the eviction statistic
description: evictionsCount is semantically a count of involuntary capacity-driven evictions (used identically in browser.ts). delete() is a voluntary user operation and should not increment this counter; stats().persistent.evictions is intended to reflect cache pressure, not user-driven removals. The same counter is incremented correctly on capacity evictions at line 139 and 153 but incorrectly on explicit delete at line 179.
evidence: Confirmed at node.ts line 179: `this.evictionsCount++` inside `delete()`. Browser.ts `delete()` (line 186-193) does NOT increment evictionsCount — it calls `removePersistentEntry` which increments the counter in its `finally` block (browser.ts line 368). So there is also an asymmetry: browser delete() increments evictionsCount (via removePersistentEntry finally), making both implementations inconsistent with the finding's intent, but node is the explicit site.

title: set() for oversized entry increments evictionsCount even though nothing was evicted
description: When an entry is too large to persist (size > persistentLimit, line 137), the code deletes it from the tracker and increments evictionsCount (line 139). But if the entry was never in the tracker to begin with (freshly set, first time), no eviction actually occurred — the counter is inflated. The tracker.delete is a no-op for absent keys but the counter increments unconditionally.
evidence: Confirmed at node.ts lines 137-141: `evictionsCount++` fires unconditionally when entry is oversized, regardless of whether the key was in the tracker. A first-ever set() for an oversized key will increment evictionsCount despite no eviction having occurred, inflating the statistic.

title: reconcile() deletes from persistentTracker inside forEachOldestFirst callback while iterating the same Map
description: forEachOldestFirst iterates `this.cache.entries()` (lru.ts line 108). Inside that callback, reconcile calls `this.persistentTracker.delete(key)` (browser.ts line 403), which calls `this.cache.delete(key)` on the same Map. ECMAScript guarantees that deleting an already-visited entry during Map iteration is safe and won't crash, but deleting a not-yet-visited entry causes it to be skipped. If the LRU ordering means a not-yet-visited key matches an orphaned entry, that stale entry is silently skipped and remains in the tracker, defeating reconciliation.
evidence: ECMAScript Map iteration skips entries deleted during iteration only if they have not yet been visited. However, `this.persistentTracker.delete(key)` in the callback deletes the CURRENT key (the one just passed to the callback), which is already-visited by definition — deletion of an already-visited entry is safe and causes no skipping. The only scenario where a not-yet-visited entry could be deleted is if `persistentTracker.delete` internally deletes a different key, which it does not. The iteration is safe.

title: Memory-cache hit increments hitCount but a concurrent inflight-set await before the memory check means the memory hit path is reachable only after the await, which is not counted separately from the pre-await miss path
description: get() awaits initPromise, then reads from memoryCache (lines 103-108, counts hit). However if a caller reaches get() before init completes and init fails, the catch swallows the error and execution falls through to the memory check. This is not a logic bug per se but the miss at line 111 fires without checking whether the key could have been served by a concurrent inflightSets write that just completed — unlike the browser path which explicitly awaits inflightSets for the key first (lines 115-116). This is a secondary note to logic-2 but also applies to the browser path: a concurrent set that completes between lines 103 and 115 would be missed only if the memory cache eviction happened in the interim.
evidence: The inflightSets await is at lines 115-116, AFTER the memory check and BEFORE the inflight-get check and getPersistent call. The ordering is correct: check memory first (no await needed), then await any pending set before hitting disk. The scenario described (concurrent set completing between lines 103 and 115 while being evicted from memory) is an inherent TOCTOU in any LRU cache and not a bug. Finder's own confidence is low.

title: LRUCache has no assertion that currentSize never goes negative, making silent accounting bugs invisible
description: currentSize is decremented on delete and eviction without a floor. If a double-delete or a size mismatch between set() and the stored value ever occurs, currentSize silently goes negative, causing the eviction condition to never fire and the cache to grow unbounded. A cheap `if (this.currentSize < 0) this.currentSize = 0` guard or a debug assert would catch accounting regressions immediately. The existing test suite has no case that verifies currentSize invariants after mixed set/delete/evict sequences.
evidence: Confirmed: lru.ts `delete()` at line 81 decrements `currentSize` only when the key exists (guarded by line 80 `if (item)`), and `evictToFit` decrements at line 70. Double-delete is protected by the `if (item)` guard, so currentSize going negative requires a size-tracking bug (e.g., size passed to set() differing from the stored value). The opportunity for a floor guard or assertion is real and would catch such bugs in testing.

title: Path traversal: caller-controlled cache key reaches path.join without sanitization
description: fileNameFor() percent-encodes the key but only for characters flagged by encodeURIComponent. A key such as '../../../etc/passwd' encodes the slashes, so the resulting filename is '..%2F..%2F..%2Fetc%2Fpasswd'. However the fallback SHA-256 branch is only taken when enc.length > 200. For shorter traversal strings the percent-encoded form is used directly in path.join(basePath, name). On Linux, path.join does NOT decode percent sequences, so the literal file '..%2Fetc%2Fpasswd' is created inside basePath — safe in practice. But this analysis assumes the OS never decodes percent sequences; on some FUSE or network filesystems it might. More critically, the key is entirely caller-controlled and there is no explicit validation that basePath is an ancestor of the resolved path — a defence-in-depth gap. A path.resolve + startsWith check on the final path would close this definitively.
evidence: encodeURIComponent encodes forward slash as %2F and backslash is NOT a path separator on Linux (Windows path.join normalizes backslashes). A key '../../etc/passwd' becomes '..%2F..%2Fetc%2Fpasswd' — a single flat filename with no directory separators, which path.join places safely within basePath. The finder acknowledges the OS doesn't decode percent sequences in standard filesystems. This is a theoretical defense-in-depth gap only, not a real vulnerability for this Node.js server-side use case.

title: clear() deletes ALL files in basePath including non-cache files placed there by other code
description: clear() calls fs.readdir(basePath) and unlinks every file in the directory unconditionally. If the caller configured basePath to a shared directory (e.g. a temp dir or a data dir that also holds other assets), clear() will silently delete unrelated files. There is no check that each file's name matches the cache naming convention before deletion. An attacker who can trigger cache.clear() on a misconfigured instance can achieve arbitrary file deletion within basePath.
evidence: Confirmed at node.ts lines 193-196: `fs.readdir` returns all files, then all are unlinked without any name-format filter. `doInit()` (lines 40-56) also filters only `.tmp-` files and stats all others, showing the cache assumes exclusive ownership of basePath — but clear() doesn't enforce this assumption. Filtering to `safeCacheName`-matching names before deletion would be a safe hardening. Also documented as contracts-5.

title: Manifest JSON parsed without size/count caps — a crafted OPFS manifest can exhaust JS heap
description: loadManifest() calls JSON.parse on the raw text of the manifest file and then iterates over manifest.entries without bounding how many entries are processed. OPFS storage is same-origin so a malicious third party cannot write to it directly; however any code running in the same origin (e.g. a stored XSS payload, a compromised service worker, or developer tooling) can write an oversized manifest. Processing 10M entries or a single entry with a multi-MB 'name' field would cause unbounded memory growth. Adding a max-entry count and individual field length cap is trivial.
evidence: The threat requires same-origin code writing a crafted OPFS manifest. For this app (a JXL viewer served to end users), same-origin XSS or compromised service worker is a real but indirect threat vector. JSON.parse itself can handle arbitrary-sized data up to available heap, so DoS is possible in principle but requires an attacker with same-origin execution. The finding is real but severity depends on threat model.

title: Manifest entry.name is used as an OPFS filename without verifying it was produced by cacheNameFor()
description: When the manifest is loaded, entry.name values from disk are fed directly into persistentTracker and later passed verbatim to opfsRoot.getFileHandle(name) and opfsRoot.removeEntry(name). If an adversary can write to OPFS (same-origin XSS or a compromised service worker), they can inject a manifest with a crafted name such as '__jxl_cache_manifest.json' to cause the cache to delete its own manifest on eviction, or names designed to collide with other OPFS entries. A validation step — checking that the name matches the output of cacheNameFor() — would prevent this.
evidence: Confirmed in browser.ts lines 385-388 and reconcile()/removePersistentEntry(): `entry.name` is used as-is in OPFS file operations (getFileHandle, removeEntry) without format validation. A manifest entry with `name: '__jxl_cache_manifest.json'` would cause the manifest itself to be removed on eviction. The OPFS name format check `entry.name.length > 0` noted in contracts-4 is also absent, making `name: ''` another failure mode.

title: No validation that basePath is an absolute, resolved path before use
description: opts.basePath is accepted as-is and passed to path.join without verifying it is an absolute path or that it has been resolved to a canonical form. A relative basePath like '../secret' is quietly accepted and used for all file operations. Adding a check that path.isAbsolute(basePath) and optionally that path.resolve(basePath) === basePath at init time would catch misconfiguration early and prevent subtle traversal scenarios.
evidence: Confirmed in node.ts lines 37-39: doInit accepts basePath without absolute-path validation and immediately uses it in fs.mkdir. A relative basePath resolves relative to process.cwd() which can change, leading to different directories across process lifecycle. An isAbsolute check at construction time would close this.

title: clear() drains inflightSets/Gets maps without awaiting them, allowing in-flight continuations to mutate post-clear state
description: clear() increments _generation and then synchronously empties inflightSets and inflightGets (lines 200-201), but the Promise closures that were already in flight continue executing on the microtask queue. Those closures captured `gen` at call-time, but their `await previous` resolves AFTER clear() has already run, and the generation check at setPersistent/getPersistent level is designed to guard against this. However, the memory-side writes (memoryCache.set at line 265 in getPersistent, and line 108 in set()) happen WITHOUT a generation check — so a racing getPersistent or set() can silently re-populate memoryCache after clear() has already cleared it, making the cache appear to have entries again. The generation guard protects persistent writes but not the in-memory LRU.
evidence: Confirmed: browser.ts set() writes to memoryCache synchronously at line 147-148 BEFORE the async IIFE that checks generation (line 171). A concurrent set() that called memoryCache.set before clear() ran will not re-populate after clear, but a getPersistent() that reads from OPFS and then calls memoryCache.set at line 265 IS guarded by the generation check at line 263 — so that specific path is safe. However, the set() path's memory write at line 148 happens before any generation check, meaning a set() call received after clear() starts (but before the await initPromise completes) will write to memoryCache before clear() clears it, and the write is not reversed.

category: missing generation guard on memoryCache.set in getPersistent
title: getPersistent writes to memoryCache after an await without re-checking the generation, allowing stale data to re-populate a cleared cache
description: getPersistent() checks `_generation !== gen` at line 263 before returning the buffer, but the memoryCache.set call at line 265 is INSIDE that check — meaning if generation matches, it writes to memoryCache. However, the generation is captured before the async OPFS read; if clear() runs during that read, the generation increments, the check at line 263 catches it and returns undefined. But the memoryCache.set is guarded by the same check, so that specific path is fine. The real issue is that the generation check at line 263 covers `return` but the memoryCache.set at line 265 runs unconditionally within the truthy branch — if another clear() runs between line 263 and line 265 (impossible in single-threaded JS), this would be a race. In JS single-threaded model this is technically safe, but there is a subtler bug: the inflightGets map is cleared synchronously in clear(), so the `finally` block at line 133 (inflightGets.delete) tries to delete a key that is already gone — harmless, but the corollary is that the caller of get() who was awaiting the shared pending promise will get its result written to a freshly-cleared memoryCache via the `result` return path without realizing clear() happened.
evidence: The generation check at browser.ts line 263 `if (this._generation !== gen) return undefined` gates BOTH the memoryCache.set (line 265) AND the persistentTracker.set (line 268) — they are inside the `if` truthy branch. If clear() ran and incremented generation, the check fires and returns early, skipping both writes. In single-threaded JS, no code can execute between lines 263 and 265 without an await. The finder's own analysis concedes 'In JS single-threaded model this is technically safe.' The subtler point about inflightGets callers getting data written post-clear is correct but that is the same as concurrency-1 which is already confirmed.

title: clear() resets manifestDirty but does not cancel or await manifestPendingWrite, leaving a stale timer that will write a manifest for the cleared generation
description: When clear() runs it sets manifestDirty = false (line 202) but does not null-out or await manifestPendingWrite. The 250ms debounce timer set by scheduleManifestWrite is still queued. When it fires it calls drainManifest(), which loops while manifestDirty. Since manifestDirty was reset to false by clear(), drainManifest exits immediately without writing — so in that normal path no stale manifest is written. However if set() is called after clear() but before the old timer fires, scheduleManifestWrite returns early (manifestPendingWrite !== null) so no new timer is registered, and the old timer's drainManifest will see the new dirty flag and write a manifest with new data — which is actually correct behavior but the race window means the manifest may be written much sooner or later than expected depending on timing. More critically, if the timer fires after a second clear(), manifestPendingWrite is null (set to null in the finally) but the timer fires anyway and writes a manifest, because the finally only runs after the async chain completes — this is a task leak.
evidence: The drainManifest loop checks `manifestDirty` which clear() sets to false. writeManifest() (called from drainManifest) checks `_generation !== gen` before writing (lines 443-447), so a write from a pre-clear timer will abort due to the incremented generation. The scenario where the old timer fires after set() adds new data is actually correct: the old timer's drainManifest sees manifestDirty=true and writes the current (post-clear) state, guarded by the new generation. No stale data is written. The 'task leak' claim is incorrect because the generation guard in writeManifest prevents writing stale content.

title: get() awaits inflightSets then checks inflightGets in a non-atomic window, allowing a duplicate persistent read to start
description: In get(), after the memory-cache miss, the code awaits any inflight set (line 116) and then checks for an inflight get (line 119). Because the await at line 116 yields to the microtask queue, another concurrent call to get() for the same key can pass through the inflightGets check before either caller has registered its pending promise (line 126-127). Both callers then create separate getPersistent() calls and register them, but the second registration overwrites the first in the Map (line 126) — the first promise is orphaned (leaked as an unawaited promise), and its result (writing to memoryCache/persistentTracker) still runs to completion, meaning memoryCache gets written twice. The file is read twice from OPFS. This is a performance leak rather than a correctness bug since both reads produce the same data, but the orphaned promise also updates persistentTracker twice.
evidence: Confirmed: two concurrent get() calls both executing `await ps.catch(...)` at line 116 will both yield, then both proceed to check `inflightGets` before either has registered its pending promise. Browser.ts line 126 `this.inflightGets.set(key, pending)` will overwrite the first caller's promise with the second's. The first promise is orphaned: it runs to completion, writing to memoryCache twice and incrementing evictionsCount if persistentTracker.set triggers eviction. The double OPFS read is a real performance regression under concurrency.

category: inflight clear without await in node.ts
title: JxlCacheNode.clear() synchronously drops inflight promise maps without awaiting them, allowing racing set() closures to write files after the cache directory has been cleared
description: Same pattern as the browser variant: clear() increments _generation, clears the inflightSets map, then deletes all files from disk. But any in-flight set() closure that was already past its generation check (line 148) and is now executing the eviction loop (lines 149-155) or the tmp-write (lines 157-162) will continue to run. After clear() removes all files, the racing set() writes a new .tmp file and renames it — the file survives in the directory. Since persistentTracker was cleared, this file is now an orphan that will be cleaned up on next init() scan as a '.tmp-*' file. More critically, if the racing set() succeeds in calling persistentTracker.set() (line 161) after clear() has run, it silently re-populates the tracker with a stale entry.
evidence: Confirmed in node.ts lines 184-200: after `inflightSets.clear()` at line 190, any in-flight set() closure that already passed the generation check at line 148 will continue executing its writeFile+rename (lines 157-160) and then call `persistentTracker.set(name, true, size)` at line 161 — after the tracker was cleared. This re-populates the tracker with a file that clear() will have deleted during `readdir`+`unlink`, leaving a tracker entry pointing to a non-existent file.

category: missing generation guard on persistentTracker.set in node.ts set()
title: Node set() checks generation before eviction loop but not before final persistentTracker.set(), leaving tracker update exposed to a post-clear race
description: The generation is captured at line 144 and checked at line 148 before proceeding. However, after the eviction loop and the async fs.rename() (line 160), there is no second generation check before persistentTracker.set() at line 161. A clear() that runs while fs.rename() is awaited will increment the generation; the pending closure resumes, and because the generation check was already passed (line 148), it calls persistentTracker.set() on the now-cleared tracker — re-populating it with a stale entry pointing to a file that was just deleted by clear().
evidence: Confirmed at node.ts lines 144-165: generation is checked once at line 148 (before eviction loop), but `persistentTracker.set(name, true, size)` at line 161 runs after awaiting `fs.writeFile` and `fs.rename` without a second generation check. A `clear()` executing during either of those awaits will clear the tracker, but the resumed closure will re-insert a stale entry. This is the same root cause as concurrency-5 and makes the tracker lie about what is on disk.

category: missing generation guard on persistentTracker.set in browser.ts setPersistent()
title: setPersistent() checks generation before writePersistentFile but not before the retry persistentTracker.set() on QuotaExceededError path
description: In the QuotaExceededError retry branch (lines 298-316), after evictPersistentFraction() (line 301) and a second generation check (line 302), writePersistentFile() is awaited again (line 304). After this second write succeeds, persistentTracker.set() is called at line 306 — but there is no generation check after the second writePersistentFile await. If clear() runs during that await, the tracker is populated with a stale entry. This is the same pattern as concurrency-6 but on the browser quota-retry path.
evidence: The finder's own code excerpt shows `if (this._generation !== gen) return;` at line 305 BEFORE `this.persistentTracker.set(key, { name }, size)` at line 306. This guard IS present on the retry path. Confirmed in browser.ts lines 302-306: generation check after second writePersistentFile is present. Finder's confidence was already low and the excerpt self-refutes the claim.

title: Module-level tmpCounter is shared across all JxlCacheNode instances and mutated non-atomically
description: tmpCounter is a module-level variable incremented with ++ (read-modify-write) inside an async closure. In Node.js's single-threaded event loop this is safe for correctness since the increment is synchronous. However, if two JxlCacheNode instances are used in the same process (e.g. in tests or a multi-tenant server), they share the counter. This is harmless for uniqueness (the counter only needs to avoid collisions within a single process) but could cause unexpected coupling between instances in test isolation scenarios.
evidence: Confirmed at node.ts line 14: module-level `let tmpCounter = 0` shared across all JxlCacheNode instances. The ++ is synchronous so there is no correctness risk (no two increments interleave in single-threaded JS), but shared state between instances is real. The finding is accurate but severity is truly low — uniqueness of temp file names is the only concern and that is preserved.

title: Node set() serializes writes per-key but there is no cross-key write-ordering guarantee during eviction
description: The eviction loop inside the set() closure (lines 149-155) calls fs.unlink on the oldest entry while potentially other keys' set() closures are also running concurrently and doing the same. Two concurrent set() calls for different keys can both enter the eviction loop, both see the same 'oldest' key, and both call fs.unlink on it — resulting in a harmless double-unlink and an inflated evictionsCount. Adding a single per-instance write mutex (a queued promise chain) would serialize all eviction+write operations and eliminate this race, at the cost of slightly higher latency for concurrent writes.
evidence: Confirmed: two concurrent set() calls for different keys both enter their eviction loops. The eviction loop calls `persistentTracker.delete(oldest)` BEFORE awaiting `fs.unlink`, so the second concurrent closure will see a different `getOldestKey()` — actually preventing double-deletion of the same key in most cases. However, evictionsCount is still double-incremented for entries deleted by both paths, and the persistentTracker size accounting can diverge from actual disk state under concurrent eviction. The opportunity for a per-instance write mutex is real.

category: no cross-tab manifest coordination in browser
title: navigator.locks guards manifest writes but not eviction+write sequences, leaving multi-tab OPFS corruption possible
description: The Web Locks API is used around manifest writes (line 454), which prevents concurrent tabs from corrupting the manifest file. However, the eviction loop (evictPersistentUntilFits) and the subsequent writePersistentFile are NOT under the same lock. Two tabs can concurrently evict the same entry and write the same key, leading to double-eviction, double-write, and a persistent tracker that diverges from disk across tabs. Since OPFS is origin-scoped and shared across tabs, this is a real multi-tab correctness gap. Wrapping the evict+write sequence in a named lock (e.g. 'jxl-cache-write') would close this.
evidence: Confirmed: navigator.locks is only used in writeManifest() (browser.ts lines 454-458) to protect manifest file writes. The evictPersistentUntilFits(), writePersistentFile(), and persistentTracker mutations are not under any Web Lock. Multiple tabs sharing the same OPFS origin can concurrently evict the same LRU entry and write the same key, leaving divergent per-tab trackers. reconcile() on next init will repair, but mid-session the tracker diverges.

title: Node set() silently swallows write failures after eviction, leaving tracker out of sync
description: The inner try/catch in node.ts set() catches write errors with only a console.error and returns without re-throwing. Because the persistentTracker.set() at line 161 is inside the try block, a failure after rename but before set (or a rename failure) leaves the tracker believing the file exists when it does not. Subsequent get() calls will return undefined from disk, and has() will return true — a consistency lie. The node.ts eviction path also does not increment evictionsCount when size > persistentLimit removes the tracker entry at line 138-140 — it does at line 139 but the delete+unlink path at lines 153-154 in the loop does not update a dedicated metric.
evidence: Confirmed at node.ts lines 157-164: `persistentTracker.set(name, true, size)` at line 161 is only reached if writeFile AND rename both succeed. If rename fails, the catch at line 162 runs and returns silently — tracker is NOT updated. So the tracker correctly does NOT reflect the failed write. However, the orphaned .tmp file remains on disk (see errors-8). The description's claim about `has()` returning true for a missing file is inaccurate for the write-failure case — but the catch swallowing the error (no rethrow) means callers of set() never know the persist failed, which is confirmed as a silent data-loss path.

category: missing error handling / unhandled promise rejection
title: scheduleManifestWrite() lets drainManifest rejection become an unhandled promise rejection
description: The promise chain at line 414-417 calls .then(() => this.drainManifest()) but only wraps the cleanup in .finally(). If drainManifest() (or writeManifest() inside it) throws, the rejection propagates out of the manifestPendingWrite promise, which is stored as an instance field and never awaited by any caller. The .finally() resets manifestPendingWrite to null correctly, but the underlying rejection is unobserved — in Node.js this raises an UnhandledPromiseRejection, in browsers it fires window.onunhandledrejection. A .catch(() => {}) or explicit swallow after .then() is needed.
evidence: Confirmed at browser.ts lines 414-416: the chain `.then(() => this.drainManifest()).finally(...)` does not have a `.catch()`. writeManifest() has an internal try/catch that swallows errors (line 449-451), so in practice drainManifest rarely throws. But if writeManifest's internal catch itself throws (e.g. a bug in the catch block), the rejection propagates to manifestPendingWrite which is never awaited. Adding `.catch(() => undefined)` after `.then(...)` closes this definitively.

title: setPersistent() rethrows non-QuotaExceededError DOMExceptions, causing callers to see a rejected promise they do not handle
description: Lines 311-314 rethrow any DOMException that is not QuotaExceededError (e.g. SecurityError when OPFS is restricted by permissions policy). The only caller is the async IIFE inside set(), which does not catch this rethrow — it propagates into the inflightSets promise. The awaiter in set() (line 177 `await pending`) will then also throw, but set() itself does not have a try/catch. This means callers of cache.set() can receive unexpected rejections from transient OPFS errors that the browser impl otherwise tries to handle gracefully.
evidence: Confirmed in browser.ts lines 311-313: non-QuotaExceededError DOMExceptions from writePersistentFile are rethrown. The IIFE in set() (lines 169-173) does not catch these, so they propagate into the `pending` promise at line 174. set() awaits `pending` at line 178 without a try/catch (only a finally at line 179). The caller of set() receives the rejection. Since JxlCache.set() is typed as `Promise<void>`, callers may not expect rejections from OPFS errors — especially SecurityError which is non-transient.

title: Node getPersistent() silently swallows all read errors including non-ENOENT failures
description: The catch block at line 111-113 discards every error (permission denied, IO error, corrupted file, out-of-memory on buffer allocation) and returns undefined, treating them all as cache misses. The caller then counts this as a miss and the error is invisible. Unlike the browser variant which distinguishes NotFoundError from other DOMExceptions and logs non-404 errors, the Node variant gives no signal. Disk errors during a read should at least be logged at warn level so operators can diagnose storage failures.
evidence: Confirmed at node.ts lines 111-114: bare `catch` with no error parameter and no logging. All errors — ENOENT, EACCES, ENOMEM, corrupted file — are silently treated as cache misses and the tracker entry is deleted. Browser.ts (lines 273-277) by contrast logs non-NotFoundError DOMExceptions. A permission denied or disk full error during read would be completely invisible in Node.

title: clear() clears inflightGets/Sets maps but does not resolve/reject the outstanding promises, leaving any awaiting callers suspended indefinitely
description: clear() calls this.inflightGets.clear() and this.inflightSets.clear() at lines 199-200 while concurrent get() or set() calls may be awaiting those same promise references. After the map entries are deleted, the original pending promises are still running (they are not cancelled), but a new get() call after clear() will create a new inflight entry — so the old promises become orphaned. The orphaned promises may later write into memoryCache or persistentTracker in a new generation (the generation guard at line 263 covers getPersistent, but set()'s inline IIFE generation guard at line 171 does not cover the memory write path which already happened before set() reached the async code). This is a latent correctness issue on concurrent clear+set.
evidence: Confirmed: browser.ts clear() removes map entries without cancelling the promises (JS has no promise cancellation). Concurrent get() callers awaiting the removed promises continue to receive results from those orphaned promises — the promises still resolve and the callers receive data written in the pre-clear generation. This is the same root cause as concurrency-1 and compounds it. The 'suspended indefinitely' claim in the title is inaccurate (promises do resolve), but orphaned-promise contamination is real.

title: loadManifest() swallows JSON.parse errors and non-NotFoundError OPFS errors identically, hiding manifest corruption
description: The outer catch block at line 390 is completely bare — it catches NotFoundError (no manifest yet, benign), malformed JSON (corrupt manifest), and OPFS permission/IO errors all identically without logging. A corrupted manifest file will be silently ignored, leaving all persistent cache entries unindexed until reconcile() re-discovers them by directory scan. This is not catastrophic (reconcile() runs after), but the corrupt file is never cleaned up and will be re-parsed on every init until manually removed. A check for SyntaxError would allow targeted cleanup.
evidence: Confirmed at browser.ts lines 390-392: bare `catch { // No manifest yet. }` swallows all errors. A SyntaxError from malformed JSON, a SecurityError from OPFS permission issues, or any other error is silently ignored. reconcile() does run afterwards (line 394) and repairs orphaned entries, but a corrupt manifest file is never deleted and will cause the same silent parse failure on every init(). Logging the error type would allow diagnosis.

title: loadManifest() does not validate that entry.size does not exceed persistentLimit, allowing tracker inflation from a crafted/stale manifest
description: The manifest validation at line 386 checks entry.size >= 0 and Number.isFinite but does not cap it at persistentLimit or any reasonable maximum. A stale manifest written with an older, larger limit could populate the tracker with entries whose total size exceeds the current limit, causing the tracker's currentSize to misrepresent available space without triggering eviction (LRUCache.set accepts size > maxSize only if size > maxSize directly, but the tracker maxSize can be reduced post-manifest-load by the estimate() call which runs before loadManifest at line 93, so this specific scenario is avoided). However, an individual entry.size could be MAX_SAFE_INTEGER, making currentSize arithmetic wrap in unexpected ways in edge cases with many entries.
evidence: LRUCache.set() in lru.ts line 54 checks `if (size > this.maxSize) return` and drops entries that exceed maxSize. A manifest entry with size > persistentLimit would be silently dropped by the LRUCache, not inserted. The currentSize cannot be inflated beyond maxSize by this path. An individual MAX_SAFE_INTEGER size would be dropped immediately. The scenario of currentSize arithmetic wrap is not reachable due to this guard.

title: Node set() does not clean up the .tmp file on rename failure, leaving orphaned temp files in basePath
description: If fs.writeFile succeeds but fs.rename fails (e.g. cross-device rename on some OS configurations, or a concurrent clear() that deleted the directory), the .tmp-PID-N file is left on disk. The doInit() scan at line 45 does clean up .tmp- files on next startup, so the leak is bounded to the current process lifetime — but under heavy traffic with repeated rename failures, many orphaned temps can accumulate. A finally block that calls fs.unlink(tmp).catch() after a rename failure would be safer.
evidence: Confirmed at node.ts lines 158-163: if `fs.rename` throws, the catch at line 162 logs and returns, leaving the `.tmp-PID-N` file on disk. doInit() (line 44-46) cleans up `.tmp-` files at startup but not during runtime. Under concurrent access or repeated rename failures (e.g., EXDEV from cross-filesystem rename), orphaned temp files accumulate until the next process restart.

category: no error taxonomy / structured error types
title: No typed error class for cache failures; callers cannot distinguish QuotaExceeded, NotFound, or IOError programmatically
description: All cache errors are surfaced as bare DOMExceptions (browser) or Node system errors (node), requiring callers to inspect .name or .code strings. A small CacheError hierarchy (CacheQuotaError, CacheIOError) would make error-boundary handling in jxl-session and higher layers explicit and robust. Currently jxl-session callers have no way to know if a set() failure was a quota issue they should back off from versus a transient IO error they could retry.
evidence: Confirmed: JxlCache.set() is Promise<void> with no documented error types. Browser path rethrows DOMExceptions for non-quota errors (errors-3), Node path silently swallows read errors (errors-4). Callers in jxl-session or jxl-stream have no typed way to distinguish storage-full from permission-denied. This is a real capability gap that makes error-boundary handling fragile.

title: doInit() OPFS getDirectory() and loadManifest() have no timeout, blocking init indefinitely on frozen storage
description: navigator.storage.getDirectory() and the subsequent OPFS file operations in loadManifest()/reconcile() can hang indefinitely on some browsers when the storage subsystem is busy or the quota API is slow. Since init() is awaited (lazily) before every public method via `await this.initPromise.catch(() => undefined)`, a hung init blocks all cache operations. A Promise.race with a timeout sentinel and fallback to in-memory-only mode would bound the blast radius.
evidence: Confirmed: browser.ts doInit() awaits navigator.storage.getDirectory(), navigator.storage.estimate(), loadManifest() (which itself awaits OPFS file reads), and reconcile() (which iterates all OPFS keys). No timeout guard is present. initPromise is awaited in every public method (lines 101, 138, 145 etc.) — a hung init hangs all cache operations including get() called from the decode pipeline.

title: get() copies the memory-cached buffer on every cache hit via slice(0)
description: Every memory cache hit calls `mem.slice(0)` to return a defensive copy. For a cache serving large JXL frames (potentially megabytes each), this copy occurs synchronously on the calling thread on every hit. If callers immediately transfer or consume the buffer (e.g. postMessage with transfer), they could accept a zero-copy view instead. The copy is not guarded by any size threshold, so even 10 MB frames are always fully copied.
evidence: Confirmed at browser.ts line 107: `mem.slice(0)` on every memory cache hit. The copy is necessary to prevent caller mutation from corrupting the cache, but it is unconditional regardless of buffer size. Node.ts has the inverse bug (logic-3) — it returns without copying. The copy cost is proportional to frame size and could be avoided with a documented contract that callers must not mutate the returned buffer.

title: set() unconditionally copies the input buffer before storing it in the memory cache
description: At line 147, `buffer.slice(0)` makes a full defensive copy of every buffer written. This is done even for callers that have already detached the buffer (e.g. post-transfer callers won't use it) or for callers that do not alias it after calling set(). The copy doubles peak memory consumption during a write and adds latency proportional to buffer size. The copy in `getPersistent` (line 265) additionally copies from the OPFS-read buffer before storing in the memory cache, resulting in two live copies momentarily.
evidence: Confirmed at browser.ts line 147: `buffer.slice(0)` before memoryCache.set. The CLAUDE.md invariant states `postMessage(msg,[pixels]) DETACHES the ArrayBuffer` — callers that transfer the buffer to a worker before calling set() would pass a detached (zero-byte) buffer. A transferred buffer has byteLength 0 so the copy would be trivial, but a direct-write caller that owns the buffer exclusively still pays a full copy. The copy is necessary for safety without a contract guaranteeing caller non-aliasing.

title: getPersistent() copies the OPFS-read buffer twice: once into memoryCache, once as the return value
description: After reading from OPFS, `buffer` is stored directly in `memoryCache.set(key, buffer, ...)`. Then on line 271, `buffer.slice(0)` makes a second copy to return to the caller. This means for a miss that hits OPFS, there are two full copies of the data in memory simultaneously. The correct pattern is to store the buffer in the cache and return a copy, OR to store a copy and return the original — never copy twice.
evidence: Confirmed at browser.ts lines 261-271: `buffer` (from `file.arrayBuffer()`) is stored in memoryCache at line 265, then `buffer.slice(0)` at line 271 creates a second copy to return. Both are live simultaneously at peak. The fix is to store `buffer.slice(0)` in the cache and return the original `buffer`, or vice versa — one allocation instead of two live copies.

title: getPersistent() in Node creates three copies of the file data
description: Node's `getPersistent` reads a file into a Node `Buffer`, then calls `.buffer.slice(...)` to get the underlying ArrayBuffer view (copy 1 if the underlying pool is shared), then calls `.slice(0)` again on that ArrayBuffer (copy 2, stored as `master` in memoryCache), then returns `master` directly (no third copy, but `master` is the one in the cache — meaning the caller holds a reference to the cached value). Actually the caller gets `master` which is the same object stored in `memoryCache`, so a mutation by the caller would corrupt the cache. This is inconsistent with the browser implementation which returns a copy.
evidence: Confirmed at node.ts lines 105-110: `buffer.buffer.slice(...)` creates arrayBuffer (copy from Node's pool buffer), then `.slice(0)` creates master (second copy). Master is stored in memoryCache AND returned to the caller (same reference — no third copy, but aliased). The two copies are redundant: the intermediate `arrayBuffer` is discarded. Correct fix: use `master = Buffer.from(buffer).buffer` or allocate once. Also the alias issue is the logic-3 bug.

title: cacheNameFor() calls crypto.subtle.digest (async SubtleCrypto) on every long-key lookup
description: When a key's encoded length exceeds 200 bytes, `cacheNameFor` calls `crypto.subtle.digest('SHA-256', ...)`. This is an async operation that dispatches to the Web Crypto engine on every call. The result is never memoized: `setPersistent`, `getPersistent`, and `removePersistentEntry` all call `cacheNameFor(key)` independently for the same key, so a single cache `set()` call that triggers eviction could hash the same key many times. A per-session in-memory Map<key, name> would reduce this to zero repeated hashes.
evidence: Confirmed: cacheNameFor is a module-level free function with no memoization. setPersistent calls it at line 287, getPersistent at line 250, removePersistentEntry at line 360 — three separate calls possible per cache operation for the same long key. Each invokes the async Web Crypto engine. For short keys (<= 200 chars encoded) the synchronous path is taken, so this only affects long-key scenarios, but those would see 3x the hashing overhead per operation.

title: SHA-256 hex encoding uses spread + map + join, allocating N+1 intermediate arrays
description: The hex encoding `[...new Uint8Array(d)].map(b => b.toString(16).padStart(2, '0')).join('')` creates a spread array of 32 elements, a mapped array of 32 strings, and then joins them. A simple `for` loop appending to a string or using `Array.from` with a pre-allocated array and a single join would allocate far less. This path is taken for long keys only but is still unnecessarily wasteful each time it runs.
code_excerpt: return 'sha256-' + [...new Uint8Array(d)].map(b => b.toString(16).padStart(2, '0')).join('');
evidence: Confirmed at browser.ts line 46: spread `[...new Uint8Array(d)]` creates a 32-element Array, `.map(...)` creates another 32-element Array of strings, `.join('')` concatenates. Three allocations for 32 bytes of SHA-256 output. A `Array.from(new Uint8Array(d), b => b.toString(16).padStart(2,'0')).join('')` would reduce to two allocations. This is a micro-optimization but the finding is factually correct.

title: evictPersistentUntilFits() evicts one entry at a time with sequential awaits in a while loop
description: Each iteration of the eviction loop calls `removePersistentEntry` which awaits `opfsRoot.removeEntry()`. OPFS `removeEntry` calls are independent and could be batched via `Promise.allSettled`. Under a cache pressure scenario where N files must be evicted before a new entry can be written, this serializes N filesystem calls sequentially. For large eviction runs this adds noticeable latency.
evidence: Confirmed at browser.ts lines 335-343: sequential `await removePersistentEntry(oldest)` in a while loop. Each call awaits `opfsRoot.removeEntry()` independently. Since the number of entries to evict is determined by the size check, the set of entries to remove could be pre-computed (collect all entries to evict from the tracker, then `Promise.allSettled` all `removeEntry` calls). This would be significantly faster under cache pressure.

title: Node set() evicts entries sequentially with awaited fs.unlink calls in a while loop
description: The eviction loop in Node's `set()` awaits each `fs.unlink` one at a time before checking the next LRU entry. Since the entries to evict are already known at the start of the loop (persistentTracker has all the accounting), the unlinking could be done in parallel. Serialized unlinking adds O(eviction count) * disk-latency overhead before a write can proceed.
evidence: Confirmed at node.ts lines 149-155: `persistentTracker.delete(oldest)` and `evictionsCount++` happen synchronously before `await fs.unlink`, so the size accounting is updated before each async call. This means the evict-candidates can be pre-collected (track all names to unlink) and then `Promise.allSettled` the unlinks. Same pattern as performance-7 on browser.

title: writeManifest() serializes the entire persistentTracker on every coalesced write, even for small deltas
description: Each manifest write calls `forEachOldestFirst` to build an entries array from the full tracker, then JSON.stringify, then TextEncoder.encode, then writes the entire file. For a cache with thousands of entries this is O(N) CPU work per write, triggered on every set/delete/eviction. An append-log or delta-write approach would reduce this to O(1) per mutation. The 250ms debounce reduces frequency but not the per-write cost.
evidence: Confirmed at browser.ts lines 434-444: full O(N) serialization of persistentTracker on every manifest write. The 250ms debounce coalesces writes but if the cache has N entries and each set() triggers a write, the total work is O(N × write_frequency). For a cache with thousands of entries serving a gallery of JXL images, this is measurable. The finding is real but the fix difficulty is correctly marked complex.

title: Node doInit() stats every file in the cache directory individually via Promise.all(files.map(stat))
description: At initialization, `doInit` reads the directory and then issues a parallel `fs.stat` for every file found. For a large cache (e.g. 10,000 entries), this fans out to 10,000 simultaneous `stat` syscalls. While they run in parallel, the overhead of creating 10,000 promises and saturating the libuv thread pool can cause significant startup latency and memory pressure. A concurrency-limited approach (e.g. p-limit or chunked batches) would be more robust for large caches.
evidence: Confirmed at node.ts lines 42-51: `Promise.all(files.map(...stat...))` fans out one async stat per file. For large caches this is N simultaneous libuv thread-pool tasks. The finding is real but practical impact depends on cache size — for typical JXL caches with hundreds of entries the startup cost is negligible. For large installations it could be significant.

title: stats() does not expose per-operation latency or OPFS read/write timing
description: The stats object tracks hit/miss counts and eviction counts but has no timing data. Given that OPFS reads and writes are the dominant latency source, exposing p50/p99 read and write durations would allow callers to detect when persistent storage is degrading. This is an opportunity to add lightweight running-average timing (using a few counters, no extra allocations) alongside existing stats.
evidence: Confirmed: stats() returns counts and sizes but no timing. For a cache whose primary performance concern is OPFS latency (can be 10-100ms per read on some browsers), the absence of read/write timing makes it impossible to diagnose storage degradation from stats alone. A running sum of read latency and count would suffice for a simple average without extra allocations.

title: cacheNameFor() creates a new TextEncoder instance on every call
description: The `cacheNameFor` function instantiates `new TextEncoder()` each time it is called for the SHA-256 path. TextEncoder instances are lightweight but not free. The class already has `this._encoder` as a reusable TextEncoder on `JxlCacheBrowser`, but `cacheNameFor` is a free function so it cannot use it. Either make it a method or use the module-level singleton. This is minor but affects every long-key lookup.
code_excerpt: const d = await crypto.subtle.digest('SHA-256', new TextEncoder().encode(key));
evidence: Confirmed at browser.ts line 45: `new TextEncoder()` inside `cacheNameFor`, a module-level function that cannot access `this._encoder`. A module-level `const _encoder = new TextEncoder()` singleton would eliminate the per-call allocation at zero structural cost.

title: JxlCache.stats() is typed as `any`, hiding a shape mismatch between Browser and Node implementations
description: The `JxlCache` interface declares `stats(): any`, which means the compiler cannot catch the fact that `JxlCacheBrowser.stats()` returns a `persistent` object with `evictions` AND `quotaEvictions`, while `JxlCacheNode.stats()` returns a `persistent` object with only `evictions`. Any consumer that reads `stats().persistent.quotaEvictions` gets `undefined` silently when backed by the Node implementation. The fix is to define a concrete `CacheStats` type covering the union of both shapes (with `quotaEvictions?: number`) and replace `any` on the interface.
evidence: Confirmed: browser.ts line 17 `stats(): any`. Browser stats() returns `persistent.quotaEvictions` (line 233), Node stats() returns no `quotaEvictions` (lines 211-217). TypeScript cannot flag callers reading `stats().persistent.quotaEvictions` on a JxlCache reference. Shape divergence is real and compiler-invisible.

title: JxlCacheNode.stats() omits `quotaEvictions` present in JxlCacheBrowser.stats(), breaking consumers that depend on the Browser shape
description: Both implementations satisfy `JxlCache` (which says `any`), but any consumer created via `createJxlCache()` that reads `stats().persistent.quotaEvictions` receives `undefined` on Node without a type error. The two `stats()` shapes are structurally diverged: Browser has `{ evictions, quotaEvictions }` while Node has only `{ evictions }`. This is a real data-contract mismatch for any telemetry or UI that surfaces quota stats.
evidence: Confirmed: node.ts stats() lines 203-223 omits `quotaEvictions`. This is a duplicate finding with contracts-1 (same root cause: `stats(): any` + shape divergence). Both are valid but the fix closes both simultaneously.

title: has() returns true for stale tracker entries without verifying the OPFS file exists, breaking the contract that has(k) == true implies get(k) !== undefined
description: After `reconcile()` runs at init, `persistentTracker` is in sync with OPFS. However, between init and a `has()` call, the OPFS file could have been removed externally (e.g., OS storage pressure, storage.clear(), another tab's cache). `has()` trusts `persistentTracker.has(key)` without hitting the filesystem, so it returns `true` but a subsequent `get()` returns `undefined`. Callers that skip `get()` when `has()` returns false-positive waste no work, but callers that make decisions from `has()` (e.g., skipping an expensive re-encode) will incorrectly believe the data is available.
evidence: Confirmed at browser.ts lines 137-142: `persistentTracker.has(key)` returns true without any OPFS file existence check. External OPFS modification (another tab's cache.clear(), browser storage pressure eviction, or manual devtools deletion) can cause has() to return true while get() returns undefined. This is documented as a weak guarantee common to LRU caches, but if callers rely on has()→get() ordering without re-checking get()'s return, they can proceed with undefined data.

title: loadManifest() does not reject manifest entries with empty name, allowing a corrupt manifest to overwrite OPFS root directory handle on removeEntry('')
description: The manifest validation guard at line 386 checks `typeof entry.key === 'string'`, `typeof entry.name === 'string'`, and `Number.isFinite(entry.size)`, but does not check `entry.name.length > 0`. A crafted or corrupt manifest with `name: ''` will pass validation, be inserted into `persistentTracker`, and later cause `opfsRoot.removeEntry('')` to be called during eviction or reconcile — behavior of `removeEntry('')` is undefined (may throw, may remove the root). Adding `entry.name.length > 0` to the guard closes this.
evidence: Confirmed at browser.ts line 386: `typeof entry.name === 'string'` passes for empty string. An entry with `name: ''` passes validation and is stored in persistentTracker. When removePersistentEntry(key) is called during eviction, it calls `this.opfsRoot.removeEntry('')` at browser.ts line 363. The FileSystem API behavior for `removeEntry('')` is implementation-defined and likely throws a TypeError or removes nothing — either way it's an unintended operation. Fix: add `entry.name.length > 0`.

title: JxlCacheNode.clear() deletes ALL files in basePath, not just cache-managed files, violating the assumption that basePath is exclusive to the cache
description: clear() calls `fs.readdir(basePath)` and then `fs.unlink` on every file, including files that were not written by this cache instance. If `basePath` is accidentally shared with application data, or if two JxlCacheNode instances share a directory, clear() will silently delete non-cache files. The Browser implementation has the same pattern for OPFS, but OPFS directories are typically isolated. The contract should either (a) restrict deletion to files whose names match the `safeCacheName`/`sha256-*` naming pattern, or (b) document that `basePath` must be an exclusive cache directory.
evidence: Confirmed at node.ts lines 193-196: all files are unlinked unconditionally. This is a duplicate of security-2 but framed as a contracts violation rather than a security issue. Both are valid: it's simultaneously a security risk (attacker-triggered clear) and a contract gap (no documentation that basePath must be exclusive). The fix is the same: filter to cache-managed filenames before deletion.

title: JxlCacheNode.doInit() silently treats persistent=true with no basePath as a no-op, but set() later writes to disk when basePath is provided mid-lifecycle
description: The `CacheOptions` interface marks `basePath` as optional. When `persistent: true` but `basePath` is `undefined`, `doInit()` returns without initializing the file store. However, `set()` checks `this.opts.persistent && this.opts.basePath` independently — if a caller mutates `opts.basePath` after construction (opts is readonly in practice, but the field on `CacheOptions` is not `readonly`), `set()` would begin writing files that `doInit()` never created the directory for. More practically: callers that pass `{ persistent: true }` without `basePath` get silent no-op persistence with no error or warning, which is a contract surprise. A validation check or warning at construction time would make the contract explicit.
evidence: Confirmed at node.ts lines 37-61: `{ persistent: true }` without `basePath` silently initializes as memory-only without warning. The `CacheOptions` interface (browser.ts lines 3-8) has `persistent?: boolean` and `basePath?: string` as independent optional fields, making this combination legal with no type error. A warning at construction time or a TypeScript discriminated union requiring basePath when persistent=true would close the contract gap.

category: no API schema / typed return contract
title: No exported CacheStats type — stats() shape is undocumented and compiler-invisible, making it impossible to write typed consumers
description: The `JxlCache` interface declares `stats(): any`. Neither implementation exports a named type for the return value. Any consumer that reads hit rates, eviction counts, or quota info for dashboards or tests must redeclare the shape inline or cast. Exporting a `CacheStats` interface (or a discriminated union `BrowserCacheStats | NodeCacheStats`) would let the compiler catch future shape changes and allow callers to write typed telemetry code.
evidence: Confirmed: no `CacheStats` or equivalent type is exported from browser.ts or node.ts. The finding is an opportunity (not a bug) and the same root cause as contracts-1/2. All three are fixed together by defining and exporting a CacheStats type.

title: JxlCache interface has no close()/flush() method, so a pending scheduled manifest write is silently dropped on page unload
description: scheduleManifestWrite() defers the actual OPFS manifest write by 250 ms via setTimeout. If the page navigates or is closed during that window, the manifest write never fires. On next load, reconcile() will clean up orphaned files, but any entries added since the last successful manifest write will be lost from the tracker (their files remain on disk but are treated as orphans and deleted). Adding a `close(): Promise<void>` method to the interface that flushes `manifestPendingWrite` and drains the manifest would allow callers to persist cleanly before unload via a `beforeunload` or `pagehide` handler.
evidence: Confirmed: JxlCache interface has no close() or flush() method. A page unload during the 250ms debounce window will drop the manifest write. reconcile() on next init will re-discover files, but traversing OPFS keys is O(N) and slower than reading the manifest. The missing close()/flush() method is a real lifecycle gap, especially for a gallery app where users navigate frequently between images.

title: JxlCacheNode.get() waits for inflightSets before serving persistent read in Browser but not in Node, creating a subtle behavioural divergence
description: In `JxlCacheBrowser.get()` (line 115-116), before issuing a persistent read the code awaits any in-flight set for the same key (`const ps = this.inflightSets.get(key); if (ps) await ps`). `JxlCacheNode.get()` does not do this: it proceeds to `getPersistent()` while a concurrent `set()` might still be writing the tmp file and renaming it. On Node this means a `get()` racing a `set()` can return `undefined` (the old file is gone, the rename not yet complete) even though a `set()` was just issued for that key. The behaviour of the two JxlCache implementations diverges for concurrent callers.
evidence: Confirmed and matches logic-2 finding. Node.ts get() (lines 63-96) has no reference to inflightSets. Browser.ts get() (lines 115-116) awaits inflightSets. This is the same root cause finding duplicated across two detectors. The behavioral divergence is real and means the JxlCache interface has an undocumented concurrent-access contract difference between implementations.
